coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots based solid phase microextraction of 2-nitroaniline followed by fluorescence sensing for selective early screening and sensitive gas chromatography-mass spectrometry determination
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shuqin
Yang, Huangsheng
Yang, Shenghong
Huang, Yiquan
Xiang, Zhangmin
Ouyang, Gangfeng
coredata.dc:description: For decades, researches have been involving in improving and optimizing solid-phase microextraction (SPME) technique to fit the growing environmental analysis demand. But it is more significant now to introduce meaningful information into the analysis line instead of improving every step in SPME. Herein, we design a carbon dots (CDs) based SPME platform and report its unique application in selective early screening and sensitive detection of 2-nitroaniline. The fluorescence quenching state of the CDs fiber after extraction could be used to prejudge whether an environmental sample contains 2-nitroaniline exceeding the maximum permitted level (30 μg L−1) regulated by the Chinese Ministry of Environmental Protection or not. Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) is believed to be the primary quenching route that leads to electron transfer from the electron-rich CDs to the electron-deficient analyte. A preliminary in vivo study on Daphnia magna and a 2-nitroaniline assay in environmental water and biofluid samples suggested the safety and the excellent qualitative ability of the proposed platform. As a result, the innovative incorporation of fluorescence sensing and SPME not only achieves the information imbedding of environmental analysis system, but also combines the advantages of two techniques including early visual-screening, reusability (≥50 times), high specificity and excellent quantitative ability (detection limit: 0.011 μg L−1). We believe this study can open new avenues in the field of environmental toxicant monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-phase microextraction
Selective early screening
Carbon dots
Nitroaniline
Fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recognition selectivity of the solid state CDs
Fabrication of a fluorescent SPME device
Synthesis and characterizations of the nitrogen-rich CDs
Evaluation of the fluorescent SPME device
Evaluations of the proposed sample pretreatment approach

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Guangdong Provincial Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photos of the N-doped CDs in solid and aqueous states; (b) TEM image and (c) particle size distribution of the N-doped CDs; (d) AFM image, (e)height profile and (f) statistical height distribution of the CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum and (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the N-doped CDs.
(a,d) Photos, (b,e) PL spectra (excitation wavelength, 420 nm) and (c,f) quenching selectivity of the solid state CDs with addition of mix-analytes and single analyte.

Zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime of the CDs before and after addition of 2-nitroaniline. (The insert images are fluorescence lifetime profiles. The error bars for zeta potential represent the standard deviation determined from seven independent assays.)

SEM images of a fluorescent CDs fiber at (a) 800 × and (b) 1500 × ; (c) comparison of extraction efficiencies of the CDs fiber under three different conditions; photos of the CDs fiber (d,e) before and (f) after extraction of 2-nitroaniline: (d) under visible light, (e,f) under a 365 nm UV beam. (d–f) The right fiber was used for enrichment experiment of 2-nitroaniline and the left fiber was used for blank comparison.

(a) Comparison of LODs for nitroanilines assay; (b) comparison of extraction efficiency of a CDs fiber with commercial PA and PDMS fibers; (c) Survival rate of Daphnia magna under exposure of untreated AFW and treated AFWs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots based solid phase microextraction of 2-nitroaniline followed by fluorescence sensing for selective early screening and sensitive gas chromatography-mass spectrometry determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Engineering and Technology Research Center for Ambient Mass Spectrometry, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Emergency Test for Dangerous Chemicals, Guangdong Institute of Analysis (China National Analytical Center Guangzhou), Guangdong Academy of Sciences, 100 Xianlie Middle Road, Guangzhou, 510070, China

Guangdong Institute of Analysis (China National Analytical Center Guangzhou)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Aquatic Product Safety/KLGHEI of Environment and Energy Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Aquatic Product Safety/KLGHEI of Environment and Energy Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of Aquatic Product Safety/KLGHEI of Environment and Energy Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For decades, researches have been involving in improving and optimizing solid-phase microextraction (SPME) technique to fit the growing environmental analysis demand. But it is more significant now to introduce meaningful information into the analysis line instead of improving every step in SPME. Herein, we design a carbon dots (CDs) based SPME platform and report its unique application in selective early screening and sensitive detection of 2-nitroaniline. The fluorescence quenching state of the CDs fiber after extraction could be used to prejudge whether an environmental sample contains 2-nitroaniline exceeding the maximum permitted level (30 μg L−1) regulated by the Chinese Ministry of Environmental Protection or not. Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) is believed to be the primary quenching route that leads to electron transfer from the electron-rich CDs to the electron-deficient analyte. A preliminary in vivo study on Daphnia magna and a 2-nitroaniline assay in environmental water and biofluid samples suggested the safety and the excellent qualitative ability of the proposed platform. As a result, the innovative incorporation of fluorescence sensing and SPME not only achieves the information imbedding of environmental analysis system, but also combines the advantages of two techniques including early visual-screening, reusability (≥50 times), high specificity and excellent quantitative ability (detection limit: 0.011 μg L−1). We believe this study can open new avenues in the field of environmental toxicant monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the forefronts in environmental analysis science is to minimize and even eliminate the tedious sample preparation procedures [1–3]. In this context, as a solvent-free and green equilibrium-based sample pretreatment technique that integrates sampling, isolation, and enrichment into one step, solid-phase microextraction (SPME) was invented to address the need for rapid sample preparation both in the laboratory and on-site where the investigated system is located [2–6]. In this technique, a small amount of extracting phase dispersed on a solid support is exposed to the sample for a well-defined period of time and then transferred to the injection port of an analytical instrument (e.g. gas/liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, mass spectrometry, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy, electrochemistry and etc.) to complete desorption and analysis procedures [2–4].

Since first proposed in 1990 by Arthur and Pawliszyn, SPME has undergone rapid developments and it has become a prior choice for applications in food, environmental and life sciences [5–8]. Therefore in the last few decades, innumerable researches on the principle [2,9], automated and assistive systems [10–12], breakthroughs of detection limits [5,13], novel adsorbents [13–15] and diverse applications [7,8] of SPME continually optimized this popular technique. As is known, researchers around the world have kept pursuing the ideal combination of automation, micromation, integration, greenization and informatization into a single environmental analysis procedure. SPME has almost achieved all these goals. Nonetheless, during the whole analytical process, we can not obtain any direct information about the sample until the results appear on the analytical instrument. To this end, instead of improving the performance of every step in SPME, it is significant to introduce more meaningful information into the analysis line.

Inspired by the advantages of visual identification technique [16–20], it is supposed that early visual-screening of a sample in SPME technique can be achieved by adopting fluorescence sensing into the analytical system. A most simple and direct way to achieve the incorporation of two techniques is to exploit a fluorescent sensor as the target response and enrichment material of SPME, which means the fluorescent material should capture and response to the target analyte in solid state. However, aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), a main problem for fluorescence sensors (e.g. small-molecule organic dyes, inorganic quantum dots, carbonaceous nanoparticles, fluorescence polymers), is the primary obstacle that hinder the integration from realization [21,22]. Recently, Hu et al. reported an effective strategy to tackle the ACQ dilemma by building branched and rigid substructures, where the sterically crowded branch segments help weaken the ACQ effects in a way of suppressing the intermolecular interactions in solid state [23]. Among all the fluorescence sensors, carbon dots (CDs) with an inner graphitic core and an outer shell composed of carboxylic and oxygen/nitrogen based groups [24–27], shows a great potential to be tailored as a proper responsive adsorbent of SPME without ACQ effect.

Given this, we designed a new sample pretreatment platform via integrating fluorescence sensing and SPME techniques for the first time. In this contribution, the N-doped CDs, functionalized by multiple oxygen and nitrogen groups with green fluorescence in both aqueous and solid states, were exploited to fabricate a fluorescent SPME device. The CDs-based SPME fiber exhibited selective recognition and sensitive determination performance for 2-nitroaniline (a carcinogenic and mutagenic contaminant commonly appears in environmental systems). The tunable fluorescence quenching state of the CDs fiber after extraction was investigated and optimized to prejudge the concentration level of 2-nitroaniline. The quenching mechanism was explored through zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system. A preliminary in vivo study on Daphnia magna was conducted to prove the safety of the proposed device. Finally, the fluorescent SPME platform was utilized to the early screening and the accurate quantification of 2-nitroaniline in environmental water samples and biofluids.

Unless otherwise stated, all starting materials and reagents were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. First, 3.0 g of calcium citrate, 3.0 g of urea and 10 mL of ultrapure water were mixed and stirred to form a suspension. Then the heterogeneous solution was heated in an 800 W domestic microwave oven for 6 min and a yellow clustered solid of N-doped CDs was obtained. The CDs were then heated at 60 °C for 1 h. The crude product was dissolved in ultrapure water and then the solution was filtered through a 0.20 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities. The N-doped CDs solid was obtained by the solvent evaporation of the CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition selectivity of the solid state CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Standard solutions of mix-analytes and single analyte were prepared and stored at 4 °C before use (see details in the Supplementary Information). To investigate the recognition selectivity of the CDs solid, 2.0 μL of each standard solution was added into the CDs solid respectively. Fluorescence images (under 365 nm UV irradiation) and fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength, 420 nm) before and after addition of different analytes were obtained to evaluate the fluorescence quenching effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of a fluorescent SPME device
ce:section.ce:para: A facile direct-coating method [5] previously proposed by us was utilized to fabricate the fluorescent CDs-based SPME fiber (see details in the Supplementary Information). Before use, the prepared fiber was conditioned at 250 °C for 60 min in the GC injection port under nitrogenous atmosphere to avoid any contamination or carryover. The analyses of the organic compounds were conducted by using direct immerse or headspace mode of SPME, coupled with a gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS).
Experimental details, materials and instruments are available in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the nitrogen-rich CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped CDs in this work were prepared through a one-pot microwave heterogeneous reaction between calcium citrate and urea, where calcium citrate served as the carbon source and urea acted as the nitrogen source. As an active substance, urea first decomposed and produced large amount of ammonia due to fast and efficient heating under microwave [28–31], which subsequently accelerated the decomposition of calcium citrate and prevented non-uniform nucleation growth [24]. The thermal carbonization of the precursors and the nucleation occurred in succession. As a result, the nuclei grew by the diffusion of other molecules toward the surfaces of the nanoparticles [32]. The obtained N-doped carbonaceous nanoparticles exhibited good aqueous solubility and green fluorescence both in solid and aqueous states (Fig. 1 a).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1b) demonstrated that the N-doped CDs were uniform in size with an average diameter of about 1.6 nm and exhibited a nearly spherical shape. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis (Fig. 1c) also agreed with the above result. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image and height profiles of the CDs material (Fig. 1d–f) confirmed its average height of 2.4 nm, which was higher than that in the TEM image. It indicated that the CDs were partly aggregated due to the changes of relative humidity or dispersing agent in the AFM measurement [33].

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurements further confirmed the chemical structure and composition of the N-doped CDs (Fig. S1). The 30.46% nitrogen and 38.10% carbon proved that the CDs were both nitrogen-rich and carbon-rich (Fig. S1a). The peaks at 284.0, 285.0, 286.1, and 289.0 eV in the C1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) were ascribed to sp3 C–C/sp2 CC, sp3 C–H, sp3 C–O/C–N, and sp2 O–CO/CN bonds, respectively [34,35]. The N1s peaks (Fig.S1c) at 399.2 and 400.2 eV revealed that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of C–N–C (sp3) and (C)3–N (sp3), respectively [34]. The O1s peaks (Fig. S1d) at 531.0 and 532.0 eV were assigned to CO quinone-type groups and C–OH phenol groups and/or C–O–C ether groups, respectively [36]. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 2 a) also agreed with the above discussion. Characterization results from FTIR and XPS reveal that the basic substructure of the as-synthesized CDs is the heptazine heterocyclic ring unit [37].

Optical properties of the solid state CDs were studied by photoluminescence and UV–visible spectroscopic measurement (Fig. 2b). The prepared N-doped CDs showed absorbance at ∼240 nm due to π-π∗ transition of the CC bonds in the sp2 bonded region and at ∼330 nm due to n-π∗ transition of the CO bonds in the sp3 bonded regions [38]. The absorption peak centered at ∼420 nm corresponded to the optimum excitation wavelength of the solid state CDs. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the material showed a peak at 520 nm (Fig. S2) was measured under the optimum excitation wavelength (420 nm).

Selectivity is a critical parameter to evaluate the performance and practicability of the CDs. To preliminarily investigate the fluorescence response of the N-doped CDs toward different substances (Fig. S3), standard solutions of mix-analytes and single analyte were added onto the surface of the solid state CDs, respectively. First of all, the effects of the used solvents were tested (Fig. S4), and no visible change occurred. Secondly, mix-analytes were tested and the mix-aniline sample exhibited obvious quenching effects on the fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. 3 a). The mix-aniline sample contains eight different compounds. So thirdly, eight compounds in the mix-aniline sample were investigated individually. The results (Fig. 3d) showed that only three nitroaniline isomers exhibited obvious quenching effects on the N-doped CDs. More detailedly, the quenching effects of all the samples were characterized by PL emission spectra (Fig. 3b,e). Here we can draw two conclusions: (1) the solid state CDs possess unique quenching selectivity for nitroaniline isomers (Fig. 3c,f). Nevertheless, the CDs are not selective to each of them (Fig. 3f). (2) The quenching effect of 2-nitroanline on the CDs is higher than that of 4-nitroanline, and followed by the quenching effect of 3-nitroanline (Fig. 3f).

In the formation of the N-doped CDs, urea acted as a nitrogen source that contributed to positive surface functional groups like amino groups, while calcium citrate functioned as the carbon source and provided negative surface functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. As shown in Fig. 4 , the zeta potential of the CDs aqueous solution was −13.41 mV, which implied the negative charge on the CDs’ surface. Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) can be the primary quenching route that can lead to electron transfer from the electron-rich architectures to the electron-deficient analyte [38,39]. The existence of abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the CDs and the negatively charged CDs surface can play effective roles in hydrogen bonding interaction and electrostatic interaction with the –NH2 group of nitroaniline and consequently resulting in quenching of emission intensity [40,41]. As with increasing concentration of analyte, the average lifetime values are changing gradually. In the absence of analyte, the average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was ∼5.86 ns, which decreased to ∼5.00 ns and ∼4.93 ns in the presence of 2-nitroaniline with different concentrations (insert images in Fig. 4 and Table S1). The result suggests that both static and dynamic quenching processes take place in the sensing system [39]. Additionally, when 2-nitroaniline was deposited onto the surface of the CDs, with the increase of the adding amount of 2-nitroaniline, the zeta potential increased from negative charge to slightly negative charge (−1.38 mV) and then to neutral charge (−0.15 mV), suggesting the strong interaction between the CDs and 2-nitroaniline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the fluorescent SPME device
ce:section.ce:para: We have found that the N-doped CDs possessed unique quenching response toward nitroaniline isomers. However, the non-specificity of the quenching effect hinders a further application of the material for quantifying nitroanilines. The introduction of SPME technique provides a solution to improve the specificity of the CDs by properly utilizing the intrinsic properties of nitroaniline isomers. To validate this idea, a CDs-based SPME device was fabricated and carefully investigated. Herein, a neutral cure silicone sealant, whose main component is polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), was chosen as the auxiliary for attaching the CDs to the pretreated stainless steel fiber via a direct-coating method (Fig. S5). Optical image and SEM images (Fig. 5 ) of the CDs-coated SPME fiber were recorded. The CDs-based SPME fiber exhibited green fluorescence (Fig. 5e) and the thickness of the coating calculated by the SEM image (Fig. 5a) was about 15 μm. High magnification SEM image (Fig. 5b) showed that the surface of the coating was covered with the well-distributed nanoparticles of the N-doped CDs.

Subsequently, the sensitivity and selectivity of the CDs-based SPME fiber toward three nitroaniline isomers were investigated and modified. When the extraction experiment was conducted under direct immerse mode, the CDs fiber could simultaneously enrich three nitroanline isomers (Fig. 5c). The fiber displayed better adsorption affinity toward 2-nitroaniline when it was immersed into the sample matrix containing the same amounts of three nitroaniline isomers. But the specificity still was not realized. Considering that three nitroanline isomers possess different Henry’s Law constants (
K H
) [2,11], which decide the affinity of a compound for the headspace, the extraction mode was altered from direct immerse to headspace. As expected, under headspace mode, the CDs fiber exhibited excellent extraction selectivity to 2-nitroaniline (Fig. 5c, Headspace 01), which possesses a much higher
K H
value (5.90 × 10−8 atm m3 mol−1) than 3-nitroaniline (7.91 × 10−9 atm m3 mol−1) and 4-nitroaniline (1.26 × 10−9 atm m3 mol−1).

However, the enrichment performance of the CDs fiber toward 2-nitroaniline sharply decreased after altering extraction mode. So secondly, we optimized the headspace extraction conditions carefully to obtain a better extraction efficiency (Fig. S6). After these two steps, the sensitive and selective enrichment of 2-nitroaniline by the fluorescent CDs fiber was realized (Fig. 5c, Headspace 02). It should be noted that the selective enrichment of 2-nitroaniline in the matrix containing the same amounts of three nitroaniline isomers has not been reported before. Under the optimal conditions, fluorescence quenching of the CDs fiber (Fig. 5e and f) occurred after extraction of a solution containing excess amount of 2-nitroaniline. In order to realize early-screening of the fluorescence SPME device, we use dichotomy to find out the quenching point with a precision f 1 μg L−1. First, we tried a concentration of 30 μg L−1 and fluorescence quenching occurred on the SPME fiber. Then 2-nitroaniline concentrations of 15 μg L−1, 22 μg L−1, 26 μg L−1, 24 μg L−1, 25 μg L−1 were tried in sequence and finally the quenching point of 25 μg L−1 was confirmed (Fig. S7). In other words, we can prejudge weather the concentration of 2-nitroaniline in a sample exceeds 25 μg L−1 from the fluorescence of the CDs fiber, after extraction process and before obtaining specific quantitative result from an analysis instrument. The early visual-screening result is expected to be used to decide whether a sample should be further quantified or not, which can help save detection resources.

As mentioned above, the fluorescent CDs-based SPME device could be used to prejudge the concentration level of 2-nitroaniline and the fluorescence quenching point appeared at 25 μg L−1. However, the maximum permitted level of aniline compounds in industrial sewage regulated by the Chinese Ministry of Environmental Protection is 30 μg L−1 [42]. In order to use the proposed fluorescent SPME device to prejudge whether an industrial sewage sample exceeds the maximum permitted level, we need to adjust the fluorescence quenching point from 25 to 30 μg L−1.

In SPME technique, when the sample volume is large enough, the amount of extracted analyte can be calculated from this equation:
(1)
n =
K
f s
V f
C 0
where n is the number of moles of analyte extracted by the coating,
K
f s
is the distribution coefficient of the analyte between the fiber coating and sample matrix,
V f
is the volume of the fiber coating and
C 0
is the initial concentration of analyte in the sample [3,4].

In actual fact, when the fluorescent CDs fiber reached the quenching point, the amount of extracted analyte ( n ) is a certain value. From equation (1), if we want to keep the value of n unchanged while increase the value of
C 0
, we need to decrease the value of
K
f s
. In headspace SPME,
K
f s
can be transformed as:
(2)
K
f s
=
K
f h
K
h s
where
K
f h
is the distribution coefficient of the analyte between the fiber coating and headspace and
K
h s
is the distribution coefficient of the analyte between the headspace and sample matrix. Therefore, the adjustment of the fluorescence quenching point from 25 to 30 μg L−1 can be realized by decreasing the values of
K
f h
or
K
h s
. Extraction conditions that affect
K
h s
include temperature and salting while
K
f h
is only influenced by temperature. So in this work, since we have found out the quenching point of 25 μg L−1 under the optimal conditions (Fig. S6), we first fixed the GC response at these conditions. Secondly, we changed the concentration of analyte from 25 to 30 μg L−1, and re-optimized the extraction temperature or/and salt concentration to find out a new combination of conditions that the GC response at these conditions equals to the fixed one. By this means, we found two combinations of conditions (Table S2) that could realize our purpose and we chose the first one to conduct the following applications.

Under the above chosen conditions (Table S2), the analytical performances of the proposed method for the determination of 2-nitroaniline, including linear range, coefficient of determination (R2), limit of detection (LOD) and reproducibility, were investigated (Table S3). As can be seen, the proposed method exhibited wide linear range, low LOD and good reproducibilities. Then it was applied to the early-screening and sensitive determination of 2-nitroaniline in environmental water samples, including river water, pond water, wastewater in dyestuff factories, and urine from factory workers and other persons (Table S4 and S5).

After sample pretreatment (extraction process), we found that none of the water samples could make the fluorescent CDs fiber quench, which implied that none of them contained 2-nitroaniline more than 30 μg L−1. Indeed, in practical environmental applications, if a tested sample is confirmed not to contain excessive amount of target analyte, further detection can be saved and the detection resources will be offered to the samples with excessive contaminant. Consequently, time and resource are saved, and desired assessment results are obtained as well. But in this study, in order to verify the accuracy and feasibility of the proposed approach, all the real samples were quantified precisely. Further analysis results (Table S6) demonstrated that all of the wastewater collected from two dyestuff factories contained specific amount of 2-nitroaniline (1.19–7.16 μg L−1). Water samples collected from the river near these factories also contained certain amount of 2-nitroaniline (0.39–0.56 μg L−1). For comparison, water samples collected from the Pearl River and the pond water in our campus far away from the factories were analyzed as well. As a result, 0.12 μg L−1 of 2-nitroaniline was detected in the pond water while no analyte was found in the Pearl River sample. The spiked recoveries of 2-nitroaniline in nine water samples were in the range of 84.4%–120.4%, confirmed the accuracy and precision of the proposed method.

For the analyses of urine samples, the standard curve we established for analyzing environmental water samples was utilized and the matrix effect of urine sample was tested. Herein, the urine sample collected by a volunteer was first analyzed by the proposed method. Subsequently, different amounts of 2-nitroaniline were spiked into the urine samples and the spiked recoveries were between 94.7% and 118.4% (Table S7), which indicated that no obvious matrix effect was observed in urine analyses. As a result (Table S8), five of the nine workers contained 2-nitroaniline in their urine samples, while one of the four persons in our campus had 2-nitroaniline in his urine sample. Good recoveries (88.9%–126.8%) were also obtained in thirteen urine samples. It should be noted that the data here could not be a direct evidence for evaluating profession dangers in dyestuff factories. Nonetheless, the approach proposed in this work can be used for the continuous monitoring of nitroaniline in factories, effluents or biofluids as needed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluations of the proposed sample pretreatment approach
ce:section.ce:para: Indeed, numerous researches have involved the detection of nitroaniline isomers in the past decade. Compared with other reported works, considerably lower LOD of nitroaniline was obtained in this study (Fig. 6 a and Table S9), which reflected the excellent sensitivity of the proposed approach. Moreover, under the re-optimized conditions (Table S2), the self-made CDs fiber showed much better extraction efficiency to 2-nitroaniline than the commercial polyacrylate (PA) and PDMS fibers (Fig. 6b).

In addition, a preliminary in vivo study on Daphnia magna was conducted to investigate the safety of the fluorescent CDs-based SPME device. Daphnia magna were cultured under untreated artificial freshwater (AFW), AFW contaminated by CDs aqueous solution, and uncontaminated AFW that underwent an extraction experiment using the CDs fiber. In contaminated AFW, 30% of the Daphnia magna died after exposure to the CDs for 84 h, while 93% and 96% of the Daphnia magna survived in the uncontaminated AFW and the AFW after SPME (Fig. 6c). Although the CDs aqueous solution did not show significant toxic effects like some toxic substances [6], the avoidance of discharging them into the environment should be persisted. The in vivo experiment suggests the proposed fluorescent SPME device may prove advantageous to avoiding the toxic effect brought by the aqueous fluorescence detection method.

In summary, a novel fluorescent SPME device based on N-doped CDs was proposed for selective and sensitive determination of highly toxic 2-nitroaniline in environmental water samples and biofluids. The CDs-based SPME fiber displayed green fluorescence and high selectivity to 2-nitroanile even in the presence of equal amounts of its two other isomers. The proposed approach was proved to be much superior to the commercial available devices and the previous works. Notably, it is the first time that fluorescence sensing and SPME are integrated as a new sample pretreatment platform. After sample enrichment process, the concentration level of 2-nitroaniline in the matrix can be prejudged by the fluorescence of the SPME device, which shows its potential to be used as a sample selection method in practical environmental applications. Unlike the traditional fluorescence detection method, the sensing material of the proposed device will not be dissolved into the sample matrix, which consequently avoided complicated separation procedure and secondary pollution. The safety and effectiveness of the device, proven by the in vivo study with Daphnia magna and the contaminant assay in water and urine samples, suggest that it may provide an innocuous alternative for environmental toxicant monitoring.

Shuqin Liu: Conceptualization, Software, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Huangsheng Yang: Validation, Investigation. Shenghong Yang: Methodology. Yiquan Huang: Data curation. Zhangmin Xiang: Resources, Supervision. Gangfeng Ouyang: Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the GDAS’ Project of Science and Technology Development (No. 2020GDASYL-20200103047) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21806189, 21707171, 21677182).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.03.024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The challenge of micropollutants in aquatic systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. P. Schwarzenbach, B. I. Escher, K. Fenner, T. B. Hofstetter, C. A. Johnson, U. von Gunten, B. Wehrli, The challenge of micropollutants in aquatic systems, Science 313 (2006) 1073-1077.
J. Pawliszyn, Handbook of Solid Phase Microextraction, Chemical Industry Press: Beijing, 2009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in solid phase microextraction and perspective on future directions
